content:
I have a way to unify Arc and ARef but it requires min_specialization.

Currently Rust ref-counted objects are Arc<T> and C (always) ref-counted objects are ARef<T> (e.g., ARef<Task>). With min_specialization we can make them both Arc<T>, which I think is a nice unification of the concepts. This is also something that @Gary Guo has been asking for for a long time.

How do you all feel about this? Is it worth taking a new unstable feature?

I'm not sure even min_specialization is sound. Full specialization definitively isn't. In any case I don't expect it to be stabilized in it's current form and would honestly love for specialization to be demoted to optimization-only. As such please don't use specialization.
How did you intend to use specialization to unify them by the way?
Because Arc::drop needs to be specialized? Also clone
Would it work to have a WithRef type containing reference counters and data and implementing AlwaysRefCounted and then make Arc<T> an alias for ARef<WithRef<T>>?
That's what I suggested in the last meeting

Gary Guo said:

That's what I suggested in the last meeting

Indeed, but I don't think this is ergonomic because we go from Arc<T> to Arc<WithRef<T>> for Rust types.

My approach with min_specialization is to have a RefCounted trait with unsafe inc_refand dec_ref that require callers to ensure that that instance of Self is ref-counted. There would be a default implementation for all types that assumes that the object was allocated as an ArcInner (or WithRef, however we decide to call it). The C wrappers that are always refcounted specialize RefCounted to call the appropriate C functions (e.g., get_task_struct/put_task_struct for Task).

Then Arc<T> requires T: RefCounted and its constructors always allocate with ArcInner (as it is today), so it's safe to call inc_ref/dec_ref because the object was either allocated with ArcInner and uses the default impl of RefCounted, or it came from C and has a specialized RefCounted.
I don't think that work well with unsizing.
You can't unsize a ARef<Inode> to Arc<dyn Any>

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

I have a way to unify Arc and ARef but it requires min_specialization.

It seems to me it might be a bit early to adopt min_specialization. It looks like it's not functional yet?

As far as I understand, the feature works at least to the extent the standard library uses it (which is min_specialization plus a couple extensions). If we end up using it, we would need to ensure it is sound for our use case, similar to the situation with other features Arc uses (e.g. CoerceUnsized is unsound).
Even libstd managed to mess up specialization implementations to cause unsoundness in several occasions.

• https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/85873 "TrustedRandomAccess optimization for Zip containing vec::IntoIter is unsound with how specialization currently behaves around HRTB fn pointers"
  • https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/85863 "iter::Fuse is unsound with how specialization currently behaves around HRTB fn pointers"
  • https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/85322 "The implementation of InPlaceIterable for Peekable is unsound"
  • https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/81740 "Soundness issue in Zip::next() specialization"

Even just enabling min_specialization without actually using it is currently unsound:

  • https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/79457 "With min_specialization enabled, an incomplete impl for a non-static type will delegate method calls to a less-specific impl with a 'static bound" (not yet fixed)
As usual, thanks a lot @bjorn3 for all the references, very useful. I will add them in a new entry to keep track of them.
Apart from the last one all of them are resolved. My point is that even rustc devs mess up with min_specialization.

Yeah, well, that is why we have some experts around ;) Jokes aside, I think Wedson's idea is to only use it for the Arc particular case, rather than allow everyone to use it within the kernel crate (enforcing this will be easier when the crate is split). Would that be reasonable? We could also speak with upstream Rust to see what they think. Since we are anyhow in a special situation with Arc, it may make sense to put all together and give Arc (and only Arc) those special powers.
Personally I prefer no specialization to be used, but I won't stop you.
By the way how is specialization intended to help here? Who will specialize what trait?

It might not be clear to someone reading the code how this feature works without going deep, so it would make this code more difficult to review and maintain. If, in addition, the feature is difficult to apply without messing up, even more reason not to do it.
@bjorn3 Noted at https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/354, please let me know if that sounds about right to you.
Thanks!

bjorn3 said:

By the way how is specialization intended to help here? Who will specialize what trait?

From a previous message:
My approach with min_specialization is to have a RefCounted trait with unsafe inc_refand dec_ref that require callers to ensure that that instance of Self is ref-counted. There would be a default implementation for all types that assumes that the object was allocated as an ArcInner (or WithRef, however we decide to call it). The C wrappers that are always refcounted specialize RefCounted to call the appropriate C functions (e.g., get_task_struct/put_task_struct for Task).

Then Arc<T> requires T: RefCounted and its constructors always allocate with ArcInner (as it is today), so it's safe to call inc_ref/dec_ref because the object was either allocated with ArcInner and uses the default impl of RefCounted, or it came from C and has a specialized RefCounted.

Having specializing impls for C structs means you have to enable min_specialization for every crate that implements RefCounted. Also each RefCounted impl has to be either in the crate defining RefCounted or in the bindings crate defining the C struct.
It's still unclear how unsized types would be handled.
